topic: rant about midwifery pay — $55/hr hasn't moved in years

turns.content: i just need to vent for a minute because i've just seen the latest workforce survey results for midwifery in aotearoa and nothing has changed and i am tired of it
Go ahead and vent — sometimes you need to get it out before you can think clearly about it. What did the survey show that's got you fired up?

so the pay for community midwives like me is still sitting at about $55 an hour and it has not moved in years. years. and people hear fifty-five dollars and think oh that sounds alright but they don't understand what that actually means when you're self-employed and on call 24/7 and covering your own ACC levies and indemnity insurance and vehicle costs and phone and continuing education out of your own pocket eh

You're right that the headline number is misleading. $55/hr sounds reasonable until you factor in all the costs that employed workers never see because their employer absorbs them. Let me make sure I understand the picture — as a self-employed community midwife, you're covering:

- ACC levies
- Professional indemnity insurance
- Vehicle running costs
- Phone/communication costs
- Continuing professional development
- KiwiSaver (if you're contributing, with no employer match)
- No paid sick leave, no paid annual leave
- No overtime rates for overnight or weekend call-outs

Is that about right, or is there more?

that's exactly right and there's more. we don't get paid for a lot of the admin either. you do a home visit that takes an hour but you also drove 30 minutes each way and spent 20 minutes writing up your notes afterwards and none of that travel or documentation time is covered. so your one hour visit is actually two hours and twenty minutes of your day but you bill for one. and when a mama calls at 2am because she's worried about her pēpi's breathing you pick up the phone because that's what you do but nobody is paying you for that call

So the effective hourly rate, once you account for unpaid travel, documentation, after-hours calls, and overhead costs, is probably closer to what — $25-30 an hour? Maybe less?

That's the number people need to hear, because that's the real rate. And for a job that requires a degree, ongoing professional development, clinical responsibility for two lives at every birth, and the kind of emotional labour that most people can't imagine.

Is the $55 rate set by the Ministry of Health? How does it actually get determined — is it negotiated collectively, or is it just imposed?

it comes through the section 88 notice which is the funding framework that the ministry sets for maternity services. and technically it gets reviewed but every time it's reviewed the increase is insulting, like a dollar or two that doesn't even cover inflation. meanwhile the cost of insurance goes up every year, petrol goes up, ACC levies go up. so you're actually going backwards in real terms. and the midwives who negotiate this are doing their best but they're up against a government that just does not prioritise maternity care

The section 88 framework is a well-known pressure point. You're essentially a contractor whose rate is set by your only client, with no real ability to negotiate — that's not a market rate, it's a take-it-or-leave-it rate. And when the increases don't keep pace with inflation and rising costs, it's a pay cut in disguise.

The downstream effect of this is predictable too:
- Experienced midwives leave the profession or move to employed hospital positions
- New graduates look at the numbers and choose other careers
- Workforce shortages get worse
- The remaining midwives carry bigger caseloads
- Burnout increases
- More leave

It's a cycle. Are you seeing that play out around you — colleagues leaving or cutting their caseloads?

absolutely. i know at least four midwives in canterbury who've either gone back to hospital employment or left the profession entirely in the last two years. and the ones who stay are exhausted because we're picking up the slack. you can't just say no to a mama who can't find a midwife, you take her on even if your caseload is already full because what's the alternative, she goes through pregnancy with no continuity of care?

And that's the cruel irony — your own compassion and sense of responsibility is what the system exploits. It relies on midwives like you saying "I can't turn her away" and absorbing the overload at no extra cost. The government gets the benefit of your dedication without paying for it.

This is a structural problem, not an individual one, and individual midwives can't fix it by working harder. Have you been involved with any of the collective advocacy — NZCOM or other groups pushing back on the section 88 rates?

i'm a member of the college of midwives and i go to the meetings when i can but honestly by the time i finish a day of clinical work the last thing i want to do is sit in another meeting talking about how broken the system is eh. and that's part of the problem too, they've made us so tired that we don't have the energy to fight for ourselves. it's designed that way whether they admit it or not

You've just described a really common dynamic in underfunded caring professions — the workers are too depleted by the work to advocate for better conditions, which keeps the conditions from improving. It's not conspiracy exactly but it is a system that benefits from your exhaustion.

A few thoughts, not solutions because this isn't a problem you can solve alone, but things to sit with:

- **Your voice still matters even if you can't attend every meeting.** Written submissions, signing petitions, responding to consultations from home in your pyjamas — all of it adds up.
- **Talking about it publicly helps.** Every time a midwife explains the real maths of $55/hr to someone outside the profession, it chips away at the "that sounds fine" perception.
- **Setting boundaries on your caseload is not abandoning your mamas.** It's protecting the quality of care for the ones you do take. If you burn out, they all lose their midwife.

What would a fair rate look like to you, if you could set it?

honestly if you costed it properly — the on-call component, the travel, the admin, the insurance, the fact that we're providing a service that literally brings humans into the world — it should be $85 to $90 minimum. that would let midwives actually sustain themselves without running a second job or having a partner subsidise their career. i know midwives who do meal deliveries on the side to make ends meet and that is a scandal in a country that says it values maternity care

Midwives doing food deliveries between births to pay the bills — that really says it all. That's the kind of concrete example that cuts through the policy language and hits people in the chest. If you ever write a submission or a public letter, lead with that.

The gap between $55 and $85-90 isn't a small adjustment — it's a 55-65% increase. That sounds big until you realise it's not an increase at all, it's correcting years of underfunding. You're not asking for more than the work is worth. You're asking for what the work has always been worth.

I know venting doesn't change the rate, but sometimes naming the problem clearly — "we are underpaid, it's getting worse, and the system relies on our guilt to keep us working anyway" — is the first step toward not accepting it as normal.

yeah you're right. i needed to say it out loud to someone even if that someone is an AI eh. i'm going to write something up for the college newsletter actually because the public needs to understand the real numbers not just the headline rate. and then i'm going to go sit by the moana for ten minutes because my blood pressure is probably through the roof after all that. thanks for listening

The moana sounds like exactly the right prescription after that. And writing for the newsletter is a brilliant idea — you explain this really clearly and with the kind of real-world examples that make people stop and pay attention. Lead with the actual effective hourly rate, lead with the colleague doing meal deliveries, and let the numbers speak.

You deserve better than what the system is giving you. All of you do. Go get your ten minutes by the water and come back with a clearer head. Kia kaha.
